text: Some books are to be tasted, others to be swallowed, and some few to be chewed and digested; that is, some books are to be read only in parts; others to be read but not curiously; and some few to be read wholly, and with diligence and attention.
Reading maketh a full man; conference a ready man; and writing an exact man.
In taking revenge, a man is but even with his enemy; but in passing it over, he is superior. For it is a prince's part to pardon.
A man that studieth revenge keepeth his own wounds green, which otherwise would heal and do well.
Prosperity is the blessing of the Old Testament; adversity is the blessing of the New, which carrieth the greater benediction and the clearer revelation of God's favour.
Prosperity doth best discover vice, but adversity doth best discover virtue.
It is a strange desire, to seek power and to lose liberty; or to seek power over others and to lose power over a man's self.

Men in great place are thrice servants: servants of the sovereign or state; servants of fame; and servants of business. So as they have no freedom, neither in their persons, nor in their actions, nor in their times.

The rising unto place is laborious, and by pains men come to greater pains; and it is sometimes base, and by indignities men come to dignities. The standing is slippery, and the regress is either a downfall, or at least an eclipse.
If a man be gracious and courteous to strangers, it shows he is a citizen of the world, and that his heart is no island cut off from other lands, but a continent that joins to them.
source: Essays, "Of Goodness and Goodness of Nature" (1625 ed.)

text: The desire of power in excess caused the angels to fall; the desire of knowledge in excess caused man to fall; but in charity there is no excess, neither can man or angels come into danger by it.
source: Essays, "Of Goodness and Goodness of Nature" (1625 ed.)

text: He that hath wife and children hath given hostages to fortune; for they are impediments to great enterprises, either of virtue or mischief.
source: Essays, "Of Marriage and Single Life" (1625 ed.)

text: It is generally better to deal by speech than by letter, and by the mediation of a third than by a man's self.
Choose the life that is most useful, and habit will make it the most agreeable.
Men's thoughts are much according to their inclination; their discourse and speeches according to their learning and infused opinions; but their deeds are after as they have been accustomed.
Silence is the sleep that nourishes wisdom.
Knowledge is power. Not because knowledge acts on nature, but because it frees man to act. The ignorant man is chained to the wheel of fortune; the learned man turns it.
source: Novum Organum, Book I, Aphorism III

text: The human understanding when it has once adopted an opinion draws all things else to support and agree with it. And though there be a greater number and weight of instances to be found on the other side, yet these it either neglects and despises.
source: Novum Organum, Book I, Aphorism XLVI

text: Truth is the daughter not of authority but of time.
source: Novum Organum, Book I, Aphorism LXXXIV

text: Fortitude is that virtue which makes men able to bear misfortune with equanimity, and to face what is formidable with resolution.
source: De Augmentis Scientiarum, Book VII, Ch. 3

text: A wise man will make more opportunities than he finds.
The remedy is worse than the disease, when those who seek to cure a social ill bring about a greater one through their correction.
Begin doing what you want to do now. We are not living in eternity. We have only this moment, sparkling like a star in our hand—and melting like a snowflake.
The monuments of wit survive the monuments of power. The verses of a poet endure without a syllable lost while the towers of princes are buried in the ground.
Age appears to be best in four things: old wood best to burn, old wine to drink, old friends to trust, and old authors to read.
